A 1 -1 Carrier - Load Port Integration Notes 1 A 1-1.1 The automation handling features do not need to be molded into the plastic shell of the FFF but can be attached as a framework around the shell. Al-1.2 To increase the stability of the FFF on the KCs, it is recommended that the points on the FFF bottom extend as close as practical to the HPD to minimize tipping of the FFF. A 1-1.3 Skewness, watp, rock, and stiffness are implicitly defined in the geometric tolerances. A 1-1 .4 The position tolerance of the door of the FFF is likely to be much larger than the position tolerance of the registration pins. To make both manual and automated door opening easier, it is recommended that the holes for the registration pins on the door of the FFF have openings with a lead-in capability. A 1-1.5 If the bottom of the FFF does not extend below the bottom conveyor rail, the conveyor rail may become contaminated and may distribute particles. A 1-1.6 Although both retaining features on the bottom of the FFF shall be able to withstand a force in any direction of/60, continuously applied stress may result in plastic deformation. A 1-1. 7 In order to minimize particle generation when the FFF door is opened or closed, it is recommended that the tolerance between the FFF door and its frame be larger than the tolerance between the FFF door registration holes and FIMS registration pins. A 1-1.8 One type of carrier presence sensor uses a beam of light with an optical detector that is triggered when the beam of light is attenuated as it passes through the FFF shell. A 1-1.9 The use of the registration pins for FFF door lead-in to the load port door is not recommended. The registration pins should be only used to limit the maximum displacement of the FFF door while on the load port door. Neither the FFF nor FFF door positions should change due to engaging or disengaging the registration pins. When the load port experiences utility loss (such as EMO, vacuum loss, electrical failure, etc.), the registration pins may be used to maintain the FFF door's position, and to ensure that the FFF door does not fall off. The clearance between the registration pins and the registration pin holes should be less than the clearance between the outer edge of the FFF door and the inner edge of the FFF door frame. The diameter of the registration pin holes should be designed to accommodate the registration pin tolerance and the registration pin hole location tolerance

It may also apply to equipment edge data used to control the manufacturing equipment (e.g., recipe parameters specified by a factory host that the manufacturing equipment uses to set up for its expected operation or activity). OTICE: SEMJ Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It is the responsibility of the users of the Documents to establish appropriate safety and health practices, and determine the applicability of regulatory or other limitations prior to use. 3 Limitations 3.1 This Guide does not define specific interfaces, data transfer mechanisms, or control mechanisms that the manufacturing equipment is required to support. The guidance provided to equipment suppliers is independent of the data transfer mechanism. 3.2 This Guide does not define any specific data content that the manufacturing equipment is required to report, how it should be structured, or its data quality. Example of a Single Factory etwork-Facing Component 6.3.3 Figure 2 illustrates an alternative configuration example where, in addition to the factory network-facing component in the manufacturing equipment, the equipment supplier provides an external computing device that also provides access to some of the equipment data. For example, the equipment supplier provides an analysis server separate from the manufacturing equipment to store and analyze raw data from the manufacturing equipment. NOTE 5: External computing devices may be edge compute (bringing computing and storage resources very close to the manufacturing equipment to improve response time and save bandwidth), or they may be located on the factory network. NOTE 6: The equipment data in the external computing device may be a subset of equipment data available on the manufacturing equipment, or it may be a duplicate of the data on the manufacturing equipment. It is outside the scope of this Guide to determine the benefits of moving the data from the manufacturing equipment to the external computing device versus keeping the data in two locations. As far as this Guide is concerned, there is no distinction or requirements between these two approaches. NOTE 7: A variation of this architecture includes a group controller scenario (where an external computing device provided by the equipment supplier supports multiple manufacturing equipment on the factory floor). This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Reviews Subcommittee on June I 8, 20 I 2. Available at www.semiviews.org and www.semi.org in August 20 I 2; originally published September I 999; previously published July 2006. E This Standard was editorially modified in January 2020 to correct a reference error. A change was made to 7.8.1. 1 Purpose 1.1 Semiconductor factories require high levels of power quality due to the sensitivity of equipment and process controls. Semiconductor processing equipment is especially vulnerable to voltage sags. This Specification defines the voltage sag immunity required for semiconductor processing, metrology, and automated test equipment. This Specification strikes a balance between voltage sag immunity and increased equipment cost. R 1-10.2 In many circumstances, it is also useful to characterize the voltage sag immunity of equipment, components, and subsystems, by determmmg how far beyond the requirements a particular EUT can go (i.e., the depths and durations of all sags that the EUT can tolerate). SEMI F47-0706 (Reapproved 0812)e SEMI 1999, 2020 semr RI-I 0.3 It can be useful to report such characterizations by plotting the results on a depth-duration graph. R 1-10.4 Doing such characterization testing requires applying sags at progressively increasing depths for a particular duration until the EUT shuts down or upsets, then repeating the process at multiple durations. [SEMI E57] 4.2 conveyor rails - parallel surfaces on the bottom of the cassette for supporting the cassette on roller conveyors. 4.3 facial datum plane - a vertical plane that bisects the tape frames and that is parallel to the front side of the frame cassette (where tape frames are removed or inserted). On tool load ports, it is also parallel to the load face plane specified in SEMI EIS on the side of the tool where the frame cassette is loaded and unloaded. [SEMI E57] 4.4 frame cassette - an open structure that holds one or more tape frames. 4.5 hori=ontal datum plane- a horizontal plane from which projects the kinematic coupling pins on which the frame cassette sits. On tool load ports, it is at the load height specified in SEMI EIS and might not be physically realized as a surface. [SEMI E57] 4.6 robotic handling flange - horizontal projection on the top of the frame cassette for lifting and rotating the frame cassette. 4.7 tape frame - the frame which applies the wafer tape to the wafer and retains the wafer. 4.8 wafer tape - an adhesive plastic tape which retains the wafer or diced chip. paragraphs.context: semr SEMI G82-1115 (Reapproved 1220) SPECIFICATION FOR 300 mm LOAD PORT FOR FRAME CASSETTES IN BACKEND PROCESS This Standard was technically approved by the 3D Packaging and Integration Global Technical Committee. This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Reviews Subcommittee on August 26, 2020. Available at www.semiviews.org and www.semi.org in December 2020; originally published March 2001; previously published November 2015. 1 Purpose 1.1 This Specification defines dimensional requirements for the load port of frame cassette for 300 mm wafer in backend process equipment. It is intended to promote a uniform physical interface between equipment and the factory, to facilitate the use of automated frame cassette transport systems, and/or to meet ergonomic requirements for manually loaded equipment. 2 Scope 2.1 This is a Standard covering equipment for 300 mm frame cassette only. OTICE: SEMJ Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It is the responsibility of the users of the Documents to establish appropriate safety and health practices, and determine the applicability of regulatory or other limitations prior to use. 3 Limitations 3.1 This Standard is not intended for use in backend except frame cassette for 300 mm wafers. This Standard does not address direct loading/unloading of vacuum load locks. Requirements of such interfaces may differ from those in this Document. 4 Referenced Standards and Documents 4.1 SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines SEMI E 15 - Specification for Tool Load Port SEMI E 15.1 - Specification for 300
